data.title: Zohran Mamdani’s New York win stirs hope and uncertainty in China over future of US ties
data.description:
Supporters erupted in joy last week as Zohran Mamdani won a commanding victory in the New York mayoral race, becoming the first Muslim elected to lead the US financial capital.
The young politician’s complex identity as an African-born person of South Asian heritage, his political positions and the broader implications of his win have resonated far beyond New York. In China, his victory quickly became a talking point, both online and among scholars.
According to Chinese observers, the rise of the Democratic Party’s left wing, marked by Mamdani’s historic mayoral win, could give the party greater leverage to challenge the ruling Republicans and ease the pressure on China as the US shifts its focus inward. But it might also bring new uncertainty, they cautioned.
One academic argued that the American left’s emphasis on social justice and equality could open space for dialogue with China, fostering mutual understanding through shared policy priorities amid an intensifying rivalry.
Others were more cautious, saying that the mayoral race was primarily driven by local “bread-and-butter” issues and offered limited insight into the party’s broader direction or the future course of US foreign policy.
Mamdani, 34, was born in Uganda to parents of Indian descent and raised in New York from the age of seven. He entered the mayoral race as a little-known state assemblyman from Queens and rose to national prominence, becoming the youngest person elected as mayor of America’s biggest city in over a century.
As a self-described democratic socialist, Mamdani’s bold, left-wing platform centred on affordability, promising to improve conditions for the middle and lower classes in New York City through free bus services, a rent freeze, universal childcare and a higher minimum wage.
He defeated establishment heavyweight and former New York governor Andrew Cuomo, who ran as an independent, and Republican Curtis Sliwa on November 4.
Diao Daming, an international relations professor at China’s Renmin University, said Mamdani represented a distinct path and a “new hope” in the Democratic Party as a new-generation minority politician, and that his win, along with other Democratic victories, showed that the party had rebounded from its defeat in the 2024 presidential election.
“For Trump, it’s a clear warning, or blow,” Diao said. “With public frustration over his economic policies, the Trump administration could adjust certain policies, including tariffs, which the world, including China, should watch closely.”
US President Donald Trump previously threatened to take funding away from New York if Mamdani won and has repeatedly attacked his politics, calling him a “communist”.
The underlying driver of the Democrats’ victories last week was voters’ deep frustration with the US economy and Trump’s tariff policies, which have darkened economic prospects, Diao said.


Democrats won most of the critical races last Tuesday, including gubernatorial elections in Virginia and New Jersey as well as a vote on California’s redistricting amendment.
The sweep gave Democrats a much-needed burst of momentum after months of downbeat introspection. The party has been engaged in a long-raging debate about its way forward, with midterm primaries only months away and the 2028 presidential race already looming.
Mamdani’s victory comes as the Democrats face a historic “generational ideological shift”, with the rise of its left wing becoming an undeniable trend, according to Meng Weizhan, an associate professor at the Fudan Institute for Advanced Study in Social Sciences.
His victory underscored rising grass-roots discontent with the Democratic establishment, which has prioritised cultural and “symbolic issues” such as transgender rights – a position that had distanced the party from voters’ everyday economic concerns, Meng wrote in an article published on November 5 by the Institute of Public Policy at the South China University of Technology.
“In the next three years, the US political scene may witness a leftist transformation of the Democratic Party – a ‘metamorphosis’ that is both an inevitable trend and a necessary choice in the party’s self-rescue to win the election.”
But Diao of Renmin University said many uncertainties remained, making it unclear how local elections focused on domestic issues might affect US foreign policy or US-China relations, and Mamdani’s win did not necessarily reflect the Democratic Party’s future direction.
Meng said the rise of the Democratic left and US political polarisation presented both challenges and opportunities for China. Policies might become less predictable and ideological disputes more pronounced, yet internal US divisions could limit hardline approaches, easing the pressure on Beijing in its dealings with Washington, he wrote.
Meng suggested that China adopt a “calm and pragmatic approach while building broad connections” to prepare for “extreme and unpredictable” US policy swings amid divided politics.
“Left-wing US politicians prioritise social justice, poverty reduction and equality – areas where China has made significant achievements and is willing to share [its] experience.”
Meng added that sharing these experiences with US leftists could help promote their understanding of China’s development model and establish common ground.
China’s latest five-year plan, covering 2026-2030, lays emphasis on improving social mobility, raising the earnings of low-income groups and strengthening the middle class – as key steps towards the goal of “common prosperity” by 2050. The blueprint comes amid slowed growth and stagnant wages, which have made upward mobility increasingly out of reach for many.
Meng said China’s leadership in renewable energy could also offer a basis for cooperation with US progressives focused on climate action.
However, Zha Daojiong, a professor at the School of International Studies at Peking University, said it would be an overreach to treat similarities in domestic agendas as indicators for US-China relations.
Zha said both the United States and China had accepted limited exchanges between their societies as normal, and this was “not conducive to the long-term prospects of a solid bilateral relationship”.
“Let’s see what the pledged year-long pause to the tariffs brings,” he said, adding that more direct flights between New York and major Chinese cities could signal “a welcome sign of change”.

data.title: In Argentina, powerful explosions rock industrial area near capital
data.description:
Powerful explosions tore through an industrial estate near Argentina’s capital on Friday, sparking a huge blaze and injuring more than 20 people.
Thick billows of black smoke rose hundreds of metres into the night sky, illuminated orange from the massive blaze, observed Agence France-Presse reporters near the site, which is close to the country’s busiest international airport just outside Buenos Aires.
“The explosions and fire breaking out in the different factories are huge,” said Gaston Granados, mayor of Ezeiza, the area where the site is located.
“We are trying to control [the fire] and extinguish it but have not been able to so far,” he said on C5N television, adding that the windows of his own nearby house had been shattered and that they were evacuating families from the area.

Hospital director Carlos Santoro said his facility had received 22 injured patients.
A patient with a heart attack and a pregnant woman suffering from respiratory issues were among those admitted, he added.
Firefighters were still battling to put out the fire early on Saturday, local media reported, after authorities earlier warned it would be challenging to bring the blaze under control.
“It is a complex fire. It will be a long fire,” said Fabian Garcia, the civil defence director for Buenos Aires province.
Several companies manufacturing tyres, chemical products and other goods were located in the industrial area. Local media reported five different factories were hit by the explosions and blaze, and Agence France-Presse reporters saw that at least one factory building had been reduced to ashes.

data.title: National Games: with 3 races still to go, Zhang Zhanshuo can already be crowned swim king
data.description:
The National Games have long been billed as a proving ground for China’s new wave of athletes as the competition cycle gears up for the next Olympics, and for rising star swimmer Zhang Zhanshuo, this year’s event has become a definitive coronation.
The 15th edition of the National Games has seen the 18-year-old from Shandong sweep three major gold medals so far, including the men’s 200m, 400m and 800m freestyle events, as he establishes dominance in the middle- and long-distance categories, leaving the previous kings behind.
Zhang’s first victory, in the 400m freestyle on Monday, set the tone.
Out-duelling rivals including world-record-holding sprint champion Pan Zhanle and the legendary Sun Yang, Zhang clocked three minutes and 42.82 seconds to smash his own world junior record, set just two months ago.
He also finished almost five seconds ahead of Hubei’s Xu Haibo, who took silver, as Pan and Sun respectively took fifth and sixth places.

Still, the teenager showed deference post-race.
“I can’t say I beat them; I still need to learn a lot from them,” he said.
His strategy, he said, was not about specific countermoves against his opponents, but about looking inward.
“I just follow my own pace,” he said, before taking a moment to acknowledge his roots.
“Thank you to my parents for their support; without them, there would be no me today.”
Zhang’s second gold came from the 200m freestyle two days later. But even then, he refused to celebrate prematurely, expressing perspective rather than triumph.
“I am very happy, but there is nothing worth celebrating,” he said.
“Yesterday, Yu Zidi broke the Asian record at such a young age. I still need to learn more from others.”
Zhang was referring to the 13-year-old who won gold in the women’s 200m individual medley while breaking the event’s long-time Asian record. Her time of two minutes and 7.41 seconds erased Ye Shiwen’s 2:07.57 set at the 2012 London Olympics.
He then echoed the words of his predecessor as he reinforced his mission.
“Just as Sun Yang said a few days ago, I hope the younger generation can support the future of middle- and long-distance freestyle,” Zhang said.
And despite smashing a regional junior record, Zhang said he had his sights set on international standards.

“For me, the result achieved today is not a personal best, and there is still a significant gap compared with athletes on the world stage,” he said.
“I know I still have a lot of room for improvement on the world stage, and I cannot be complacent just because I won the championship today.
“I have already begun to reflect; having stepped off the podium means everything must start anew.”
Despite the fierce competition between them, the dynamic between Zhang and Pan has become one of mutual respect, with Chinese media quickly dubbing them the “Zhan Generation” as a representation of the future of the country’s freestyle.
Asked about the pairing, Zhang said: “He is not only my opponent, but also my idol. We have a very good relationship in private, and I will consult him if I have questions.
“I am very happy and cherish being able to compete with him today.”
This shared identity, built on mentorship and high-level competition, bodes well for China’s prospects in the freestyle relay in the years to come.
Completing his hat-trick in the 800m freestyle on Thursday, Zhang had achieved what most athletes would be proud of at the domestic level. Yet his focus remained forward-looking.
“I feel the result didn’t improve much; I still need to go back and summarise properly,” he said post-race.
Asked if winning three golds had changed his long-term goal, he said: “I’ve never thought about what would happen whether I won or not.

“I just want to always face [competition] as a challenger. I think the most important thing is to do my best and set an example.”
He added: “The competition schedule is not even half over yet; I am very much looking forward to the subsequent races.”
With three gold medals, three junior records and a mindset fixed firmly on global standards, Zhang has already delivered a historic performance.
But, as he himself cautioned, the competition is far from over.
With three more events looming – the 400m individual and relay medleys as well as the gruelling 1500m freestyle – the teenage “Triple Gold King” is primed to secure more titles and further cement his status as the new face of Chinese freestyle, perhaps beyond the national level and into the Olympics.

data.title: Who is Ahn Eun-jin, the late-blooming actress of Hospital Playlist and Dynamite Kiss?
data.description:
Korean actress Ahn Eun-jin is closing out a busy 2025 with her latest leading role this month in the romantic comedy Dynamite Kiss alongside Jang Ki-yong.
She kicked off the year by briefly returning to the Hospital Playlist role that made her famous, and a few months ago, she also appeared in the big-budget Netflix production Genie, Make a Wish, alongside Bae Suzy and Kim Woo-bin.
A contemporary of many of the leading stars of today’s K-drama industry, Ahn, now 34, took a little longer than several of her peers to get recognised.

She was a student of the Korea National University of Arts, where her classmates included Kim Go-eun, Park So-dam, Lim Ji-yeon and Lee Sang-yi, among many others.
Why we love her
Ahn’s career began on the stage in 2012, and she continued to appear in plays for several more years, although she was not confident in her abilities at first. But she persevered, gaining the strength and confidence to develop her craft.
She received positive notices for her role in the 2015 musical Infinite Energy, which ran until early 2016.

Ahn’s television career began two years later, after signing with an agency, and she immediately began to book roles. In 2019, she appeared in six series, including season one of the first Netflix Korea hit, Kingdom – she would return for season two the following year.
Ahn is also a major baseball fan. She supports the SSG Landers in Incheon and threw the ceremonial pitch for one of their games in 2023.
The star-making role
In 2020, following several rounds of nerve-racking auditions, Ahn scored the role that would cement her status in the industry: Chu Min-ha, the obstetrics and gynaecology resident at the Yulje Medical Centre in the smash hit medical drama Hospital Playlist.

While not part of the central quintet of young professors at Yulje, Min-ha – capable, sweet but still unsure of herself – swiftly became a fan favourite as one of the most relatable characters on the show.
Recognising her popularity, Min-ha returned with more screen time in the equally successful second season in 2021, by which time Ahn was already being offered leading roles.
The iconic parts
Ahn starred alongside Lee Do-hyun and Ra Mi-ran in the rural comedy-drama The Good Bad Mother, one of the most popular shows in 2023.

She plays Lee Mi-joo, the former village sweetheart of Lee’s protagonist Choi Kang-ho, a successful young prosecutor. After Kang-ho loses his memories in an accident, he returns to his village and steadily begins to reconnect with Mi-joo.
Ahn’s first leading role was in the little-seen romantic thriller The One and Only (2021-2022), which aired just after the second season of Hospital Playlist. However, the role that would truly be recognised as her leading actress breakout would come in the sweeping romantic drama My Dearest (2023).
Despite a setback with another production, Ahn nonetheless kicked off her leading lady era with flying colours in the timeless period romance alongside Namkoong Min.
In the MBC series, Ahn plays Yoo Gil-chae, a young woman from an aristocratic family who gets entangled with an enigmatic nobleman played by Namkoong. The characters are swept up in a romantic tale set against the backdrop of the Joseon era during the Qing invasion in the 17th century.

The show, released in two parts, steadily grew into a ratings hit and earned Ahn prizes from the MBC Drama Awards.
The setback was another leading role she had shot, which was initially scheduled to come out first. That was the Netflix sci-fi series Goodbye Earth, which was impacted by a drug scandal involving its other lead, Yoo Ah-in.
Yoo’s off-set woes forced producers to postpone the launch date as well as extensively re-edit the show to limit his appearance. This was largely considered to be to its detriment, as the result was difficult to follow and received scathing reviews when it quietly launched at the end of 2023.
The unheralded performances
Following her flurry of supporting roles and her big part in Hospital Playlist, Ahn finally made the transition to the big screen in 2022, taking on a supporting part in the very well-received period drama The Night Owl.

She played her first villain in the film, a role that netted her nominations at the Baeksang Arts Awards and Blue Dragon Film Awards.
She had an even bigger part in the hugely entertaining comedy film Citizen of a Kind (2024), in which she plays a young woman joining a group of women heading to China to confront a group of dangerous scammers.
Tomorrow’s turns
Ahn has had a busy 2025, with two major roles and a cameo in the Hospital Playlist spin-off Resident Playbook, returning to her beloved role as Chu Min-ha, now a professor at Yulje. Following this, Ahn currently has no new productions officially confirmed.
After a short break, she will no doubt land her next major part soon.

data.title: Vatican returns 62 artefacts connected to indigenous peoples from Canada
data.description:
The Vatican on Saturday returned 62 artefacts to indigenous peoples from Canada as part of the Catholic Church’s reckoning with its role in helping suppress indigenous culture in the Americas.
Pope Leo gave the artefacts, including an iconic Inuit kayak, and supporting documentation to a delegation of the Canadian Conference of Catholic Bishops during an audience. According to a joint statement from the Vatican and Canadian church, the pieces were a gift and a “concrete sign of dialogue, respect and fraternity”.
The items were part of the Vatican Museum’s ethnographic collection, known as the Anima Mundi museum. The collection has been a source of controversy for the Vatican amid the broader museum debate over the restitution of cultural goods taken from indigenous peoples during colonial periods.
Most of the items in the Vatican collection were sent to Rome by Catholic missionaries for a 1925 exhibition in the Vatican gardens that was a highlight of that year’s Holy Year.
The Vatican insists the items were “gifts” to Pope Pius XI, who wanted to celebrate the church’s global reach, its missionaries and the lives of the indigenous peoples they evangelised.

But historians, indigenous groups and experts have long questioned whether the items could really have been offered freely, given the power imbalances at play in Catholic missions at the time. In those years, Catholic religious orders were helping enforce the Canadian government’s forced assimilation policy of eliminating indigenous traditions, which Canada’s Truth and Reconciliation Commission has called “cultural genocide”.
Part of that policy included confiscating items used in indigenous spiritual and traditional rituals, such as the 1885 potlatch ban that prohibited the integral First Nations ceremony. Those confiscated items ended up in museums in Canada, the United States and Europe, as well as private collections.
Negotiations on returning the Vatican items accelerated after Pope Francis in 2022 met indigenous leaders who had travelled to the Vatican to receive his apology for the church’s role in running Canada’s disastrous residential schools. During their visit, they were shown some objects in the collection, including an Inuit kayak, wampum belts, war clubs and masks, and asked for them to be returned.
Francis later said he was in favour of returning the items and others in the Vatican collection on a case-by-case basis, saying: “In the case where you can return things, where it’s necessary to make a gesture, better to do it.”
The Vatican said on Saturday the items were given back during the Holy Year, exactly 100 years after the 1925 exhibition where they were first exhibited in Rome.
“This is an act of ecclesial sharing, with which the Successor of Peter entrusts to the Church in Canada these artefacts, which bear witness to the history of the encounter between faith and the cultures of the indigenous peoples,” said the joint statement from the Vatican and Canadian church.

It added that the Canadian Catholic hierarchy committed to ensuring that the artefacts would be “properly safeguarded, respected and preserved”. Officials had previously said the Canadian bishops would receive the artefacts with the explicit understanding that the ultimate keepers would be the indigenous communities themselves.
The items are expected to be taken first to the Canadian Museum of History in Gatineau, Quebec. There, experts and indigenous groups would try to identify where the items originated, down to the specific community, and what should be done with them, officials said previously.
As part of its broader reckoning with the Catholic Church’s colonial past, the Vatican in 2023 formally repudiated the “Doctrine of Discovery”, the theories backed by 15th-century “papal bulls” that legitimised the colonial-era seizure of Native lands that form the basis of some property laws today.
The statement marked a historic recognition of the Vatican’s own complicity in colonial-era abuses committed by European powers, even though it did not address indigenous demands that the Vatican formally rescind the papal bulls themselves.
The Vatican on Saturday cited the 2023 repudiation of the Doctrine of Discovery in its statement, saying Leo’s return of the artefacts concluded the “journey” initiated by Francis.

data.title: Hong Kong sees lacklustre property sales at Tuen Mun project, slowing market’s recovery
data.description:
There was lacklustre action on Saturday at the sale of the latest batch of flats at The Reserve at Gold Coast Bay in Tuen Mun, as less than a 10th of the units on offer were snapped up three hours after the launch.
Only 14 flats, of the 152 units on offer, were sold as of 6.30pm, according to agents.
“It’s almost the last of the remaining stock,” said Sammy Po Siu-ming, senior director at Midland Realty. “The sale will continue.”
The batch offered this weekend by developer Early Light International Holdings, owned by tycoon Francis Choi Chee-ming, included 86 two-bedroom and 10 three-bedroom flats, as well as 56 special units with rooftop balconies or utility platforms.
Ninety-six of the 152 units were priced between HK$4.7 million and HK$8.6 million after discount, costing about HK$10,431 to HK$15,312 per square foot. The other 56 units were put up for sale by tender.
The 14 flats that were sold consisted of two three-bedroom units and 12 two-bedroom flats, with a total value of nearly HK$100 million (US$12.9 million).
The flats offered on Saturday were part of the 1,323 phase two units at the development.
The underwhelming weekend sale failed to live up to expectations of a recovery in Hong Kong’s property market, where homebuyers have benefited from initiatives by the US Federal Reserve and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority to lower interest rates.

Property deals had surged to a three-month high in October. Sales of new and second-hand homes – as well as office units, shops, industrial properties and parking spaces – rose 4.7 per cent to 7,190 units, up from 6,870 units in September, according to data released by the Land Registry on Tuesday. That was the most since July’s total of 7,212 units.
Analysts had expected last month’s rate cut to continue to lift sentiment in the local market.
Choi was once known as Hong Kong’s “Toy King” for making die-cast Nascar collectibles and licensed dolls, including characters from the Harry Potter franchise. He turned to property development two decades ago.
Early Light started selling phase one of its Tuen Mun development, The Uppland at Gold Coast Bay, from July 2024, with the first batch of units priced at an eight-year low for the New Territories.
“Since its launch, the project’s two phases have collectively sold 1,025 units, generating over HK$4 billion in proceeds,” Early Light managing director Raymond Lee said on Saturday.
As the city’s property market showed signs of recovery, the developer had raised prices at the development between 1 and 3.5 per cent.

data.title: Defying politics: how China’s business schools are innovating to keep talent pipeline open
data.description:
Defying global geopolitical currents, China’s business schools are actively resisting the mounting risks of decoupling from the West, according to a leading accreditation body.
Its top executive said that instead of retreating into isolation amid rising US-China tensions, Chinese institutions remained deeply committed to internationalisation as they prioritised global talent and diverse cultural backgrounds to boost their competitive edge.
“No [Chinese business] school that I visited expressed resistance to international talent; instead, they actively highlight diverse cultural backgrounds to enhance global competitiveness,” said Lily Bi, president and chief executive officer of the Association to Advance Collegiate Schools of Business (AACSB).
“Chinese schools are ambitiously bridging East and West – that’s a beautiful thing, with many boasting faculties where 45 per cent or more hold PhDs from overseas,” Bi told the South China Morning Post after visiting several accredited business schools in Shanghai and Beijing earlier this month.
Founded in 1916, the AACSB is a US-based organisation with regional offices in Asia and Europe that certifies business schools. Core standards encompass leadership and resources, teaching and learning quality, academic research impact and societal influence.
It represents more than 2,000 institutions in dozens of countries. Tsinghua University’s School of Economics and Management was the first in China to achieve full accreditation in 2007, having joined as a member in 2002. Today, China has 52 AACSB-accredited business schools – second only to the United States, which has more than 500.
At least 40 additional Chinese institutions are in the accreditation pipeline, underscoring sustained demand for international validation.


China’s ascent in business education began picking up speed in the early 2000s. Propelled by the nation’s entry into the World Trade Organization in 2001 and a strategic push to modernise its economy, the government invested heavily in higher education to cultivate globally competitive talent.
The era saw the establishment and expansion of business programmes tailored to support China’s integration into international markets and the expansion of companies overseas, fostering a cadre of managers equipped for multinational operations.
“Chinese people are hard-working, ambitious and innovative,” Bi said. “Every school I visited aspires to be world-class.”
The schools benefit from robust government support – in sharp contrast to global trends. During a recent meeting with China’s education ministry, officials pledged increased resources and greater institutional autonomy, she said.
This comes as Western institutions face funding cuts and regulatory caps on tuition hikes that have not adjusted for inflation in up to a decade in some countries.
In recent years, visa restrictions and geopolitical tensions have tightened borders for international students, prompting higher education institutions – including business schools – to rethink traditional mobility models.

Speaking after a series of global engagements over the past month, Bi said that restrictions were no longer isolated to one nation but now spanned several countries in Europe and North America.
In the Netherlands, for instance, housing shortages have led authorities to cap student inflows. In other countries, international students have been increasingly conflated with broader immigration concerns, despite their distinct status.
Countries group international students together with immigrants, “even though visa rejection was historically about intent to stay, not enrolment”, she said.
This shift, she argued, overlooked the economic and cultural value students brought, including full tuition payments, diverse perspectives and cross-cultural exchanges that also enrich domestic peers.
In the US, Chinese student enrolment peaked at 370,000 in 2019 but has since fallen by about 25 per cent to around 270,000, affecting universities broadly.
But while overall international enrolment has declined, business programmes have generally appeared more resilient. The trend signals a need for adaptation, according to Bi. “You have to diversify,” she warned. “Enhance domestic recruitment, yes – but also find new ways to maintain global engagement.”
Many US institutions are already heeding the call, ensuring continuous collaboration even when physical mobility is constrained. To deliver AACSB accredited programmes in-country, they operate satellite campuses in China while others are expanding existing partnerships with local Chinese universities.
A broader shift is emerging: from moving people to moving ideas. “It used to be you move one person from one country to another,” she said. “Now, without that, you can still share ideas,” Bi said, adding that digital platforms, joint research initiatives and virtual exchanges were gaining traction.
In recent years, deans from the US, European and Chinese universities have explored dual-degree models, faculty swaps and co-developed curricula – all designed to transcend visa barriers. These efforts preserve intellectual exchanges and institutional reputations while navigating political headwinds.
Business schools are proving remarkably adaptive. “The desire for global engagement is still very strong,” Bi said, adding that “they just need to be creative”.
She described education as a cornerstone of national strength. “Everything starts from education. Building walls around students is not smart. Talent exchange benefits everyone,” she said.
While advocating for open policy, she said institutions would not just wait for the change. “They will innovate around it, ensuring that global business education remains connected, relevant and resilient.”
In China, in addition to government funding, alumni donations have surged, corporate partnerships have expanded, and lifelong learning programmes with companies have provided new revenue streams. The resilience has positioned China’s business schools favourably amid global headwinds, according to Bi.
After speaking with several deans, Bi said that Chinese schools remained focused on globalisation, preparing students for a borderless business landscape while addressing local needs at state, provincial and city levels.
“There is some concern about the direction we are travelling, but I still have hope for globalisation. Globalisation is beyond free trade. You cannot simply kill it,” she said, adding that the intertwined supply chains were evidence of irreversible interconnectedness.
Chinese business schools, which already have many partnerships, had a “strong desire” to collaborate with different parts of the world, communicate with peers, and attract and retain talent in a bid to provide quality teaching services and research output, Bi said.
Chinese schools are experimenting with models that integrate global standards with China-specific priorities, such as industry-focused curriculums. Traditional discipline silos – such as accounting, marketing and finance – are being blurred in favour of interdisciplinary, problem-oriented approaches centred on real-world industries.
One pioneering model requires every faculty member to link their expertise to at least one industry, which requires mandatory company visits. This ensures research starts with actual business challenges – not abstract theories – to make education more relevant.
AI integration is ubiquitous and strategic. “It’s not an option – it’s a strategic imperative,” Bi said.
Schools treat artificial intelligence (AI) in at least two ways: as a teachable technology – the easier part, with widespread adoption; and as a tool for enhancing operations and learning. From beginner-level implementations to advanced applications, the majority have embedded AI, reflecting the fast-evolving business world.
While China’s emphasis on innovation in its tech race with the US has steered many undergraduates towards science, technology, engineering and mathematics majors, demand for management education has remained resilient.
AACSB data from its accredited schools – representing about 6 per cent of global business schools – shows undergraduate enrolment in business growing slowly but steadily, even if not at the explosive 20 per cent annual rates of decades past.
“In terms of student pipelines, China may be getting a little bit soft on the management degree while pushing STEM,” she said, “but I think even if they do undergrad in STEM and management for the master’s degree, that’s a beautiful combination.”
“Societal strength lies in diverse perspectives. Any society should welcome that. The younger generation can pursue STEM and management – both paths are valuable,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong issues Japan travel warning citing rise in attacks on Chinese citizens
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have urged residents to exercise caution and prioritise personal safety in Japan, citing a rise in attacks against Chinese citizens in the country.
The appeal from the Security Bureau on Saturday was issued a day after Beijing told Chinese citizens to avoid travel to Japan.
“Since mid-2025, there has been an increasing trend of attack incidents against Chinese citizens in Japan,” the bureau’s updated travel alert said.
“The government reminds Hong Kong residents who intend to visit the country or are already there to exercise caution, attend to personal safety, pay attention to local announcements on the latest situation and notices on consular services from the Chinese embassy or consulate-general in Japan.”
China’s foreign ministry issued a warning late on Friday night asking Chinese tourists to avoid travel to Japan, marking an escalation in a row over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s suggestion that Tokyo could deploy its military forces in a cross-strait conflict.
The ministry’s statement said the safety of its citizens in Japan had “continued to deteriorate”, citing a series of targeted attacks this year.
The ministry said the Japanese leader had made provocative remarks on Taiwan-related issues, severely undermining the atmosphere for Sino-Japanese exchanges and posing significant risks to the safety of Chinese citizens in Japan.
It called on Chinese citizens to avoid going to Japan “in the near term”, while those already in the country were advised to “closely monitor the local security situation, enhance safety awareness and strengthen self-protection measures”.
Following the warning, Air China, China Southern and China Eastern published separate statements on Saturday, saying they would allow ticket holders to refund or change their itineraries for free for flights between Saturday and December 31. The policies only apply to tickets bought before noon on Saturday.
Japan welcomed 263,600 Hong Kong residents in April, a 42.9 per cent jump from the 184,460 visitors in the same month last year, according to the country’s national tourism organisation.
Between January and April, Japan welcomed 911,200 people from Hong Kong, a 12.8 per cent increase from the same period last year.
Last year, about 2.68 million Hongkongers visited Japan, accounting for around 7.3 per cent of the total foreign visitors to the country.

data.title: China cracks down on export of ‘zero-mileage’ cars falsely declared to be used
data.description:
China has moved to tighten regulations on used car exports, cracking down on new vehicles falsely declared to be second hand, in the latest step to ensure fair and orderly market activity.
Starting next year, exporters will have to prove after-sales service is available at the destination for vehicles that have been registered for less than 180 days, according to a notice released by the Ministry of Commerce on Friday. The availability of after-sales service must be confirmed by the car manufacturer.
It said local commerce authorities would also strengthen oversight of export licence issuance and compliance by exporters.
The new rules seek to regulate competition in the auto sector and promote healthy development of used car exports, according to the notice.
The new regulation targets the export of so-called “zero-mileage” second-hand cars – new vehicles sold under the guise of used ones – a practice that carmakers have criticised for distorting the market.
This practice emerged after China allowed used car exports in 2019 and has grown alongside the country’s expanding electric vehicle manufacturing sector, and these “zero-mileage” vehicles have gained popularity in overseas markets, including Central Asia, Russia, the Middle East and Africa, said a car exporter based in the eastern city of Ningbo who declined to be named due to company policy.


“Zero-mileage” used cars are typically sold at lower prices but without guarantee of after-sales service, he noted.
“Now Chinese carmakers are looking to develop their distribution network overseas with local dealers, so of course they oppose this kind of ‘zero-mileage’ used car export,” he said, noting the new regulation aligned with recent calls from carmakers.
Zhu Huarong, chairman of Chinese carmaker Changan Automobile, criticised the export of “zero-mileage” used cars at an industry forum in early June, saying the practice “severely damages Chinese brands’ image abroad” and had caused pricing chaos in overseas markets.
The tightened regulation meets industry expectations and gives exporters a one-month grace period, according to a social media post by the China Auto Dealers Chamber of Commerce on Friday. The group also called for exporters to make efforts to comply to ensure better long-term industry development.
About 90 per cent of the 436,000 used passenger and commercial vehicles exported by China in 2024 are estimated to be “zero-mileage” cars, according to a Reuters report from June.
The report also said “zero-mileage” exports had been a widely used tactic to inflate car sales for years.
Faced with criticism from trading partners over excess production and dumping, as well as deflationary pressure at home, Beijing has pledged to tackle “involution” across industries this year, urging the new-energy vehicle industry to rein in irrational competition.

data.title: A sea exhibition in Hong Kong explores our relationship with the ocean, a ‘complex entity’
data.description:
A bamboo-and-paper sculpture dangles from the ceiling of a gallery. It looks oddly familiar but also like nothing you have ever seen. The claws, tail and body resemble a lobster, but the horned dragon’s head, well, that is where things get a little fuzzy.
This is not a deep-sea creature but the product of a deep dive into the imagination of Hong Kong artist Louis To Wun.
Titled Seafood, the whimsical piece is part of “All-Sea: Eight Oceanic Artistic Practices from Southeast Asia and Hong Kong”, an exhibition running until January 17 at Karin Weber Gallery in Central, Hong Kong, that pays homage to the interconnection between the sea and us.
To made Seafood using the same skills he draws on to repair traditional paper effigies used in the sea goddess worship ceremonies on Cheung Chau, the outlying island where he lives. Like the part-dragon that often features in those Taoist rites, his lobster is also a chimera.
“Having lived by the sea since childhood, I often think of a lobster with a dragon head,” he says. The Mandarin word for lobster is, after all, longxia or “dragon shrimp”.

Curator Caroline Ha Thuc says “All-Sea” invites viewers to reflect on their relationship with the ocean.
“Too often, oceans are merely viewed as endless bodies of water, capable of absorbing all our excesses, providing routes for ships and offering resources for humanity,” Ha Thuc says.
The inspiration for the exhibition, Ha Thuc adds, is derived from French poet and philosopher Edouard Glissant’s (1928-2011) notion of the Tout-Monde or “All-World”, which designates a world defined by interconnection and constant transformation.
“Rather than focusing on fixed identities, it emphasises the relational processes that form and sustain those identities within a state of continual flux. This vision stresses our ability to dialogue with the unknown, not as a threat but as an enrichment, engaging the imagination to invent and renew our ways of being in the world.”
There are many reasons oceans need to be appreciated. They produce over half of the world’s oxygen, regulate climate by absorbing heat and carbon dioxide, and provide essential resources like food, jobs and transport for people all over the world.
They are also a source of minerals and potential clean energy, and they harbour compounds used to develop medicines.
However, they are still largely ignored, and their inner life, limits and principles often go unnoticed, Ha Thuc says.
“As a fluid, ever-changing and complex entity, the sea can be difficult to relate to. Seen through Glissant’s lens, the ocean becomes a living network of relationships, shaping new ways of understanding time, space and human connection.”

Ha Thuc says the featured artists have lived by the sea and developed a connection with it.
Take former Olympic sailor, Singaporean Charles Lim, whose Sea State project explores the sea’s economic, political and strategic space, while Cambodian artist Lim Sokchanlina’s photographs, which depict a blue fence adrift at sea, raise questions about the environmental consequences of container shipping. The shipping industry accounts for about 3 per cent of global greenhouse-gas emissions, surpassing even the aviation sector.
“For instance, Lim and Sokchanlina examine land reclamation, sand-dredging and extensive coastal development, practices that profoundly affect local biodiversity and marine ecosystems,” Ha Thuc says.
“These concerns mirror Hong Kong’s own developmental challenges, exemplified by the environmental impact of the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, which has, among other things, disrupted the habitat of the endangered Chinese white dolphin.”
Faris Ridzwan, who hails from a lineage of artists rooted in Malaysia’s batik tradition, reimagines this textile legacy through a contemporary lens with pieces inspired by his dives along Malaysia’s coast. His paintings Drift and Bloom and Ovular Secret draw on batik’s vivid colours but shift the focus to marine life.

Hong Kong artist Tsang Chui-mei’s paintings capture the ocean’s ebb and flow, suggesting the ceaseless negotiation between land and sea. Tide Upon the Island Shore resembles the depictions of mythical gardens in traditional Chinese ink paintings, but her method of “painting” is entirely experimental.
She first applies layers of acrylic and crackle paint on canvas, then sandpapers the dried surface to create shifting planes of colour and blank spaces that evoke the fragility of memories, the coastal ecosystem and the erasure of history.
“My piece emerged from a quiet dialogue between memory and nature, using the ocean as a metaphor for emotional depth, ecological fragility and the subconscious,” Tsang says. “The painting’s layered textures and shifting pigments evoke sedimented time and fleeting thoughts, reflecting impermanence and resilience.”
Filipino artist Joar Songcuya’s experience of the ocean is shaped by his voyages on a cargo ship to more than 86 ports. Filipinos constitute about 25 per cent of the world’s seafarers, many of whom endure job insecurity, exploitative working conditions and discrimination.
In his paintings, Songcuya portrays the sea as a perilous force and a constant companion. Tidal Apparition draws on Filipino mythology, in which the sea goddess embodies a force of creation. The Sailor and the Prophesier is a self-portrait representing the artist as a sailor.

Plastic pollution is addressed in the works of Indonesian artist Ari Bayuaji, whose woven installations are created with debris collected on Balinese beaches, while Singapore-based, Moroccan-Thai artist Juria Toramae’s video installation, Uncanny Lagoon, features fantastical marine organisms generated with artificial intelligence.
“Today, we know the long and well-documented list of the multiple impacts of climate change and anthropogenic activities on marine environments – manifesting as rising sea levels, coral bleaching, ocean acidification, fish depletion, seabed extraction, habitat loss, biodiversity degradation and various forms of pollution – industrial, plastic, chemical, acoustic,” Ha Thuc says.
“These issues are interconnected and complex, sometimes elusive and invisible to the human eye. By inviting us to engage with the sea imaginatively and emotionally, artworks can help conceptualise and visualise these issues.”
Ha Thuc adds that artists emerge as ideal mediators, bridging the global with the local, and transforming abstract ideas into concrete, sensory experiences.
“This global crisis inflicts widespread damage on both ecosystems and human communities,” Ha Thuc says.

That being said, she adds, the aim of the show is not to point to specific issues but to invite viewers to take the time to look at the sea and think of it as a living, complex entity that we have to learn to live in harmony with.
“All-Sea: Eight Oceanic Artistic Practices from Southeast Asia and Hong Kong”, Karin Weber Gallery, 20 Aberdeen Street, Central. Tel: 2544 5004. Additional events: “On The Verge” poetry reading and book launch by Hong Kong artist Claire Lee, November 27, 7pm. Curator’s tour, November 29, 4.30pm. “Our Blue Foundation: Why the Ocean Matters” by Dr Christelle Not of the University of Hong Kong, December 11, 6.30pm. “Hong Kong Marine Reef Fish Biodiversity” by Stan Shea, marine programme director at Bloom Association Hong Kong, January 8, 2026, 6.30pm. Closing party and performance by artist Alysa Leung Hoi-yee, January 17, 2026, 4pm.
